message:
perf(sync): fast-path startup auto-import probe avoids dirty-count + flush checks

Splits the read-command startup auto-import gate into two layers:

- A cheap "is JSONL newer?" probe that read commands actually need
  (`auto_import_probe_refreshing_witnesses`).
- The existing full staleness computation (dirty count, pending flush,
  db_newer) that the import path itself still runs before deciding
  whether a dirty DB should block import.

Why: read-only commands hit `compute_staleness_refreshing_witnesses` on
every invocation just to learn `staleness.jsonl_newer`. That helper
also runs the dirty-count query against `issues_dirty`, computes
`pending_export_state`, and checks `db_newer`. None of those side
states change the read-path decision — if JSONL is not newer, no
import will happen, and the dirty/flush state is irrelevant. For a
hot DB with thousands of rows, the dirty-count query is the dominant
cost on every `br ready`, `br list`, `br show`, etc.

The fast path returns the boolean `jsonl_newer` directly, with the
same opportunistic witness refresh on the side (the "JSONL bytes
match but mtime/size witnesses drifted" case), and skips the rest.

src/sync/mod.rs:

  - New `JsonlNewerProbe { jsonl_exists, jsonl_mtime, jsonl_newer,
    refresh_witness }` carrying the subset of staleness state that
    only depends on the JSONL file vs stored hash/witness metadata.
  - New `compute_jsonl_newer_impl(storage, jsonl_path) -> Result<
    JsonlNewerProbe>` that owns the JSONL-vs-stored comparison logic.
    Same algorithm as the old in-line block in `compute_staleness_impl`,
    just hoisted into a function and returning the typed probe:
      - Stored mtime witness matches → check size witness; on size
        mismatch fall back to content-hash compare; refresh_witness
        gets the observed witness when stored_hash exists but the
        size witness drifted.
      - Stored mtime witness doesn't match → check the stored
        last_import / last_export RFC-3339 timestamps; if JSONL's
        mtime is later, fall back to content-hash compare; otherwise
        the file is older.
      - Missing metadata defaults to "newer" (safe default — triggers
        an import attempt that will validate via hash).
  - `compute_staleness_impl` is now a thin wrapper over the probe:
    runs `pending_export_state`, then calls `compute_jsonl_newer_impl`
    and folds its fields into a `StalenessCheck`. No behavior change
    in the full-staleness path; `compute_staleness_refreshing_witnesses`
    still returns the same struct for callers that need it
    (`auto_import_if_stale`, etc.).
  - `auto_import_probe_refreshing_witnesses(&mut storage, jsonl_path)
    -> Result<bool>` is the new public entry point. Calls
    `compute_jsonl_newer_impl`, performs the opportunistic
    `refresh_jsonl_witness_best_effort` if a refresh witness was
    produced, and returns the boolean. Doc comment makes the
    contract explicit: this is for the read-command startup gate;
    the import path itself recomputes full staleness before
    deciding whether a dirty DB should block import.

src/main.rs:

  - Imports `auto_import_probe_refreshing_witnesses` instead of
    `compute_staleness_refreshing_witnesses`.
  - The `should_attempt_auto_import` gate now reads:
        auto_import_probe_refreshing_witnesses(...)
            .unwrap_or(true)
    If the probe errors (rare — would need a metadata read failure),
    the previous code defaulted to `staleness.jsonl_newer`'s `true`
    on the error path; the new code preserves that behavior with
    `unwrap_or(true)`. If JSONL is newer (or the probe errored),
    we proceed to acquire the sync lock and the existing
    `auto_import_if_stale` path runs the full staleness computation
    inside that lock — same as before.

Net effect for a hit (JSONL newer):
  - Probe returns true → same code path as before.
  - One extra probe call before grabbing the sync lock; the cost
    is the same as the old code minus the dirty-count + flush
    checks. Strictly faster.

Net effect for a miss (JSONL not newer, the common case for read
commands on a healthy repo):
  - Probe returns false → no sync lock, no dirty-count query, no
    pending-flush check. Dramatically less DB traffic on every
    invocation.

The opportunistic "refresh witness when bytes match but mtime/size
witness drifted" continues to fire from the new probe path on the
fast-path miss case, so the witness drift remediation that the old
code did is preserved.
